steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence opens with a long subject — 'The hypothesized protolanguage from which all Kartvelian languages have descended' — followed by a comma. That comma signals an appositive: the blank must begin with a noun phrase that renames or identifies this protolanguage, and then the sentence must have a main verb.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'the linguistic properties of the language's descendants were used...': The noun phrase 'the linguistic properties' does not rename the protolanguage — it names something else entirely. This makes the sentence a fragment with no subject for the main clause. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Proto-Kartvelian has been reconstructed by experts, who inferred the language's linguistic properties from those of its descendants.': 'Proto-Kartvelian' is a noun phrase that directly renames the subject. It is followed by a main verb 'has been reconstructed,' completing a grammatical sentence. ✓
  • Choice C — 'Proto-Kartvelian's descendants' linguistic properties were used...': The possessive noun phrase 'Proto-Kartvelian's descendants' linguistic properties' does not rename the protolanguage itself; it names the properties of its descendants. The appositive rule is violated. ✗
  • Choice D — 'the experts who reconstructed Proto-Kartvelian inferred...': 'The experts' does not rename the protolanguage. Placing it after the comma creates a dangling appositive and makes the sentence ungrammatical. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
